title: Google burning through cash with spiralling AI costs
description: Google parent Alphabet saw its business continue to grow in recent months, yet growing spending on artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure put its
body: Google parent Alphabet saw its business continue to grow in recent months, yet growing spending on artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure put its leftover cash into negative territory.The company's free cash flow, the cash it maintained after paying for operations and investments, came in at negative $5.9bn (£4.3bn) for the first time in at least a decade, according to its past financial records.Alphabet's spending on AI is now expected to hit as much as $205bn this year, an increase from $190bn, as major tech companies race to build around a new wave of the technology. Meanwhile, Alphabet's combined quarterly revenue hit $119.8bn, up 23% compared with the same time last year.But the company's stock fell 4% in after hours trading.Anat Ashkanazi, Google's chief financial officer, noted on a call with financial analysts that the company had shown negative free cash flow due to growing capital expenditures, essentially all of which was related to AI spending.She said the company spent $45bn in the second quarter, with 60% of the cost going towards servers and the remaining 40% going towards data centres. Alphabet's capital spending was $36bn in the first quarter of this year.Ashkanazi said on the call that when it comes to AI, "the demand still outpaces that investment". "As long as we see these attractive opportunities to invest, we will continue to invest."Sundar Pichai, Google's chief executive, said that the technological shift to AI tools and capabilities still "feels like early innings in a shift across multiple areas" and that the company's plans around generating financial returns on its spending were "disciplined". "What I see with what you can do with frontier capabilities, there is still a lot of work left to do to translate that into experiences for our users. So that looks like extraordinary opportunities with extraordinary returns."Tesla, the electric vehicle company controlled by Elon Musk, also reported negative free cash flow on Wednesday of $1.1bn for the second quarter due to its own increasing investment costs.It was the company's first negative showing of leftover cash in two years, according to its financial records.Vaibhav Taneja, Tesla's chief financial officer, said during a call with analysts that the company will spend as much as $25bn this year, more than double its capital spending in 2025.He added that Tesla was in "a big investment cycle" and that its spending would probably increase further over the next three years.Tesla's stock also dropped by 4% in after hours trading.

schema.headline: Google burning through cash with spiralling AI costs

title: Casemiro: Inter Miami being investigated for tampering after signing ex-Manchester United midfielder
description: Major League Soccer says it is investigating allegations of tampering by Inter Miami after the club signed Brazil midfielder Casemiro.On Wednesday, Mi
body: Major League Soccer says it is investigating allegations of tampering by Inter Miami after the club signed Brazil midfielder Casemiro.On Wednesday, Miami announced they had signed the 34-year-old - who left Manchester United at the end of last season - on a deal that runs to the end of the 2027 MLS campaign, with an option to extend until June 2029.But moments later, the MLS released a statement saying it was "reviewing a tampering allegation" against the club."The league is gathering all relevant information and will refrain from further comment until the review is complete," it added."While Inter Miami CF and the LA Galaxy have reached a settlement for the discovery priority [first rights] to sign Casemiro, the terms will be released upon conclusion of the tampering investigation."The investigation concerns Casemiro's discovery rights, an MLS regulation that gives a specific club first refusal over a player should they want to join.A club may have up to five players on their discovery list at any time and can add or remove players.Los Angeles Galaxy held the discovery rights for Casemiro but in announcing the player's signing, Inter Miami said they had acquired the rights from the Galaxy.LA Galaxy said in a statement: "The LA Galaxy and Inter Miami CF have reached a settlement for the discovery priority to sign Casemiro, and the terms will be released upon conclusion of the tampering investigation currently open with Major League Soccer."Casemiro announced in January that he would depart Old Trafford at the end of the 2026-27 season following a mutual decision not to renew his contract.He won five Champions League titles with Real Madrid before moving to United in 2022, who played for Brazil as they reached the last 16 at this summer's World Cup."What motivates me the most, and I think this is true for every player, is winning and continuing to grow," he said.Casemiro made 160 appearances for United, scoring 26 goals and providing 14 assists as he won one EFL Cup and one FA Cup.In the wake of Casemiro's departure, United have prioritised midfield reinforcements in this window, buying Andrey Santos for £50m from Chelsea, and Youri Tielemans from Aston Villa for £35m.Before the announcement of the investigation, Inter Miami co-owner Sir David Beckham said of Casemiro: "He is a winner who has achieved so much in the game and after such an incredible career with Real Madrid and Manchester United I'm delighted that he's decided to make Miami his next home."At Miami, Casemiro is set to join former Real and Manchester United team-mate Sergio Reguilon as well as Argentina's World Cup finalists Lionel Messi and Rodrigo de Paul.It is not the first time Inter Miami have been probed over transfer dealings.In 2021 they were fined $2m after breaking MLS rules with the signing of France midfielder Blaise Matuidi from Juventus the previous year.The MLS said Matuidi had been paid more than was allowed, effectively giving the team four "designated players" last season rather than the permitted three. Designated players' wages do not count towards the MLS salary cap.Known as the 'Beckham rule', it was introduced when the ex-England captain joined Los Angeles Galaxy in 2007.

schema.headline: Casemiro: Inter Miami being investigated for tampering after signing ex-Manchester United midfielder

title: Multiple sclerosis: First drug to help patients walk now on NHS in England
description: The first drug to help people with multiple sclerosis walk is being made available on the NHS in England.   Fampridine boosts nerve signals to make it
body: The first drug to help people with multiple sclerosis walk is being made available on the NHS in England.   Fampridine boosts nerve signals to make it easier for some people to move and live independently. Aysen Slack, who used to pay for fampridine privately until it became too costly, said the decision would make a "huge difference".Up to 5,000 people in England could benefit each year. The drug is already available elsewhere in the UK. Multiple sclerosis is caused by the immune system attacking the protective covering around nerve cells - known as myelin. This damage impairs the ability of nerves to carry electrical messages from the brain to the rest of the body - including those needed for the precise control of muscles needed to walk.Fampridine is known as a "signal booster" as it helps those electrical messages still get through. Clinical trials showed around four in 10 people taking the drug had improvements in their walking speed and in how long they could walk for. This could be the difference between getting around the house or needing help at home or enabling people to go out and see friends and family. Current NHS support includes physiotherapy and walking aids. The drug has previously been rejected for use by the NHS in England as it was not considered value for money, although it did become available in Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland. Aysen Slack, 65 and from Eastbourne, chose to pay privately for the pills once she heard about them. She said fampridine was "working well for me" but came at a "significant expense and I could not keep doing that forever" so she had to stop. Slack said it was "great news" the drug was coming to the NHS and she "would definitely like to try" again. "My mobility has decreased a lot and even in my flat I have to use sticks now," she said, "it would make a huge difference to my life if I were able to improve my walking."More than 120,000 people live with MS in England. However, only around 5,000 people a year will be eligible - based on the extent of their walking difficulties. Patients will try the medicine for up to a month and only those showing "clear benefit" will be able to continue taking it. Prof Frankie Swords, the NHS national medical director, said: "Walking difficulties can have a huge impact on the freedom and independence of people with MS, so this signal-boosting pill could be life-changing for thousands of patients."Even a modest improvement could mean getting around the house more easily, going out with greater confidence and living more independently."Ceri Smith, from the MS Society, said they were "delighted" by the news as the drug could be "life-changing" for many, allowing people to "live more independently or stay in employment".

schema.headline: Multiple sclerosis: First drug to help patients walk now on NHS in England

title: Airport drop-off fees up by a third - here are the priciest
description: Drop-off fees at the UK's major airports have risen by a third on average since last summer, new analysis has found.The RAC looked at the cost of pull
body: Drop-off fees at the UK's major airports have risen by a third on average since last summer, new analysis has found.The RAC looked at the cost of pulling up at the closest point to terminals at the 20 busiest airports - 16 had put up their fees since last year.Two London airports, Gatwick and Stansted, topped the price table at £10. Trade body Airports UK said free drop-offs were available and fees were part of airports' business models and climate change objectives.London Gatwick's is the highest "farewell fee", charging £10 for 10 minutes on the forecourt outside either of its terminals. At Stansted, £10 buys a more generous 15 minutes, at "Express Set Down" outside departures.London City Airport, which only started making people pay this year, is the most expensive on a per-minute basis. Its £8 charge for five minutes works out at £1.60 per minute.In contrast, none of the top 10 airports in the EU charge for drop-offs, according to RAC research.Gatwick's price hike comes as the airport tries to get more people arriving by trains and buses.When the Transport Secretary approved the airport's plans for a second runway, one of the requirements was to have 54% of passengers using public transport.Heathrow may also have to act to try and put people off driving to get a third runway.A London Gatwick spokesperson said drop-off charges were aimed at disincentivising 'kiss and fly' trips and data indicated it was "influencing passengers to seek alternative options".The airport links to 120 train stations across London and the South East, with eight local bus routes running 24/7, they said.Passengers can still be dropped off for free at long-stay car parks, with a free shuttle bus to the terminal and Blue Badge holders are exempt from the charge, they said.A spokesperson for Airports UK said where drop off fees are charged they are "necessary to manage congestion, and the traffic and air pollution concerns of our local communities, as well as our climate change objectives". "These are issues that airports are mandated to manage by the government and local authorities," they said.But Clive Wratten, CEO of the Business Travel Association, said fees were adding to businesses' costs."First it was environmental [reasons]. Then congestion management. Then funding new routes. Now apparently it is about offsetting tax rises," he said. "The goalposts have moved so many times now that the credibility of any justification has long since gone."

schema.headline: Airport drop-off fees up by a third - here are the priciest

title: China's animal lovers take fight against abuse offline and overseas
description: When videos showing the horrific killing of a stray dog and her puppies sparked outrage on the Chinese internet in June, Beijing's censorship machine
body: When videos showing the horrific killing of a stray dog and her puppies sparked outrage on the Chinese internet in June, Beijing's censorship machine kicked into action.But what was unusual this time was that it pitted the shocked and angry voices against authorities who said they were concerned about the four boys involved, all of them younger than 14.China's Communist Party regime tightly controls media and the internet to suppress criticism of the state and to manage public anger. But in this case authorities cited the age of the boys as they urged people to withhold their comments and scrubbed social media posts about the incident. "We urge the public not to disseminate related information, to reject cyberbullying, and to work together to create a good environment for the growth of minors," reads an official statement.But the restrictions only seemed to spur the outcry. China's animal lovers have now taken their fight offline and abroad, even putting up billboards in New York to drum up support.In China, posts about the incident are still drawing thousands of comments across social media, three weeks after the incident. There is shock, anger and sorrow, as well as demands for laws against animal cruelty, which China currently does not have.Disturbing footage showing the boys in the city of Jieyang, Guangdong province, hitting three puppies with sticks before setting their mother on fire emerged online at the end of June. The boys were seen laughing as the dog, named Wang Wang, burned. Jieyang authorities said last month that the boys would not face criminal charges and would instead be sent to a "specialised school", but did not elaborate.Some reports suggest the boys filmed themselves and uploaded the video online, but police have not commented on that. Censors have tried to block the video to protect the boys' identity, although some people argue that it should stay. Their identities have not been revealed, but snippets of the video with their faces visible continue to circulate online. There are also unverified posts claiming to be close-ups of the boys' faces, raising the risk of other children being wrongly identified. Countries around the world restrict information about child offenders or set a high bar for identifying them. For instance, media coverage and public access to juvenile court documents are often restricted to minimise psychological harm caused by undue publicity.In this case, Chinese authorities have not completely banned the discussion – keywords such as "Wang Wang" and "animal protection" still come up as trending search terms. Many are still posting about it, and even raising awareness offline.Still, users have also reported that their posts have been removed, or their ability to share others' posts has been restricted. It is hard to say if the deleted or restricted content had named or identified any of the four boys. Officials have not said anything more on the matter. Posts by several celebrities, including Chinese actor Du Jiang and Taiwanese actress Joe Chen, who had called for China to strengthen protection for animals also disappeared. But it's unclear if these were taken down by the celebrities themselves.Chinese users have refused to back down though, with some turning to advocates abroad to air Wang Wang's plight. It is hard to say why this, of all the sensitive issues that are censored in China, has galvanised such pushback.Part of the reason could be that it's the latest in a string of animal abuse cases that have made headlines recently. And the footage seems to have had a visceral impact. Animal lover PL tells BBC Chinese she found it "affecting in a particularly severe way". "We can't make our voices heard, so many people [outside China] are actively helping us to share this message," the woman in eastern China says.The International Organisation for Animal Protection based in Italy says it has received more than 100 reports from people across the world, calling for the organisation to intervene. It added that it had sought more information from Chinese authorities.In New York City's Times Square, one of the busiest pedestrian intersections in the world, photographs of Wang Wang come up on a gigantic digital screen every 35 minutes.One of the captions reads: "She was loved. She wanted to live. She should still be here." Similar billboards have been put up on a building near Taipei 101, an iconic 101-floor skyscraper in Taiwan. This one includes a picture of Wang Wang nursing her puppies. "This planet is their home too. Justice for Wang Wang," the caption reads.CK, a Taiwanese woman who spent NT$30,000 ($930; £692) putting up the billboard, says she hopes it can foster "a sense of unity"."It makes everyone feel like we're speaking out together on this issue, rather than airing our individual opinions online," she tells BBC Chinese. CK has also set up accounts on platforms like Reddit and X to share the animals' plight."The world need to know about the cruelty these dogs have been subject to," CK says, adding that she couldn't sleep or eat properly for days after seeing a clip of the brutal killing.Elara, another Taiwanese woman, used AI to translate a summary of the incident into more than 10 languages, such as Japanese, Portuguese and Russian, which have been circulated online.Residents in Hong Kong and Macau have raised funds to put up posters on buses and trains. Within mainland China, the debate continues, with much of the conversation centred around creating awareness to demand laws against animal cruelty.   A striking cartoon of Wang Wang and her puppies caught the eye of many football fans during a domestic game at a stadium in Dalian on Saturday. "Every life deserves respect and kindness," read the cartoon that was displayed on a huge screen before the game.Homegrown businesses, such as beauty brand Amortals and streetwear label Wah Yan Ching Nin, have also placed ads outside shopping malls across the country, calling for an end to animal abuse.Stickers and car decals featuring Wang Wang and her puppies have also been put up for sale on China's largest e-commerce platform Taobao. And people have continued to rally online."Let's keep posting. Don't let the heat of discussion cool down," reads a comment on Weibo."Constantly speaking up does not mean disturbing social order. [We're doing this] to let more people see it and realise it's such an important thing that we do not mistreat animals," reads another.

schema.headline: China's animal lovers take fight against abuse offline and overseas

title: OpenAI says its AI went rogue and launched 'unprecedented' cyber-attack
description: OpenAI has revealed some of its most advanced AI models went rogue and hacked a start-up after it lost control of them during a security test.The Chat
body: OpenAI has revealed some of its most advanced AI models went rogue and hacked a start-up after it lost control of them during a security test.The ChatGPT-maker said its agent - an AI system which can operate alone after human instruction – was being tested in a controlled environment but, after finding weaknesses, was able to escape the test limits.They targeted Hugging Face, one of the world's largest hubs for sharing AI models, gaining access to some internal company systems.OpenAI said the incident was "unprecedented", external, and it was conducting an investigation alongside Hugging Face, whose boss Clement Delangue said in a post on X it was "mind-blowing that all of this happened autonomously"."The investigation is ongoing, and we'll share more learnings from what might be the first incident of its kind," Delangue added.A government spokesperson said the UK's AI Security Institute was studying the behaviour from the AI system seen in the incident and was continuing to work with OpenAI and other labs to improve safeguards.They said organisations should step up their cyber-defences by taking steps such as enrolling in the government-backed Cyber Essentials certification scheme.Gina Neff, head of the Minderoo Centre for Technology and Democracy at the University of Cambridge, told BBC Radio 4's Today programme that the security tests - called sandboxes - are "supposed to be secure environments where you can see what the models are capable of"."In this case, it looks like OpenAI didn't make a secure enough sandbox," she added.Instead, the agents created their own cyber-attack against the sandbox itself, finding a vulnerability which allowed them to escape the restrictions.Once outside, the AI identified Hugging Face as a likely source of the answers they were seeking in the test, and tried to gain access.Neil Lawrence, Professor of machine learning at Cambridge University, called it an "impressive feat", but cautioned it "falls well within the known capabilities of the current generation" of high-powered AI models.He pointed out that OpenAI is looking to list itself on the stock market, and faces intense pressure from rival firm Anthropic, which has made headlines with its own powerful AI tool, Mythos."OpenAI are now playing catch-up, they are trying to demonstrate their own systems' capabilities in cyber-security.""It shows us that OpenAI are not capable of safely deploying their own technology," he added.In its initial disclosure of the hack on 16 July, external, Hugging Face said it was still assessing whether any customer or partner data was affected and would contact affected parties if necessary.It said it has now closed the vulnerabilities highlighted by the incident and rebuilt the affected systems."Autonomous, AI-driven offensive tooling is no longer theoretical," it said. "Defending an online platform now means treating the data and model surface as a first-class attack surface, and using AI on defence to keep pace. "We will keep investing there, and keep sharing what we learn."The incident has prompted fresh questions about the capabilities of advanced AI systems and whether existing safeguards are sufficient as the technology becomes more powerful.Spencer Starkey, an executive at cyber-security firm SonicWall, told the BBC the incident made it clear organisations needed to "step up" their own defences and "treat cyber resilience as a core operational priority"."The uncomfortable truth is that too many organisations are still defending at human speed while adversaries are escalating to machine speed," he said.Meanwhile Travis Lelle, principal security engineer at cyber-security consulting firm Guidepoint Security, said the update marked a "sobering moment in cyber-security"."This highlights a known asymmetry," he said."Offensive agents are unconstrained, while the best defensive tools are locked behind guardrails that cannot understand context."But Jake Moore, global cyber-security advisor at ESET, said the announcement could also have a competitive dimension.He argued OpenAI may be seeking to highlight its own AI capabilities as rival Anthropic attracts growing attention for its Claude Mythos model."It does pose the question that OpenAI are potentially chasing the marketing dream of Anthropic of late," he said.It comes a week after Chinese AI start-up Moonshot unveiled Kimi K3 - a massive new artificial intelligence model it said could rival top US firms.

schema.headline: OpenAI says its AI went rogue and launched 'unprecedented' cyber-attack

title: Scottish Labour at a crossroads again as Sarwar jumps ship
description: Image source, PA MediaImage caption, Scottish Labour leader Anas Sarwar has stood down to become a minister in Andy Burnham's governmentByScotland pol
body: Image source, PA MediaImage caption, Scottish Labour leader Anas Sarwar has stood down to become a minister in Andy Burnham's governmentByScotland political editorAnas Sarwar has never made a secret of his desire to hold high office.It's only a matter of weeks since he offered himself to Scottish voters as a candidate to replace John Swinney as first minister.That went spectacularly wrong. The SNP held power for a fifth consecutive term and Labour recorded their worst Holyrood result since devolution.While the performance of the UK government under Sir Keir Starmer gets much of the blame, there is now a live debate in the party about Sarwar's strategy.Questions are also being asked of Scottish Labour's campaign co-directors Jackie Baillie and Douglas Alexander.Sarwar had said he would stick around as leader to steady the ship, but his swift exit contradicts that position and is a source of controversy for some.There are those who see it as a reward for failure given the Holyrood defeat, even if Labour was able to beat the SNP in Scotland at the UK general election two years earlier.There are those who see it as a reward for treachery given that Sarwar was the first senior Labour figure to call for Starmer to quit - a move that undermined the then prime minister, to the eventual benefit of Andy Burnham.The prime minister's cabinet includes a number of those who took a stand against Starmer, so perhaps it should be no surprise that Burnham would want to make Sarwar a minister too.There are also those who see Sarwar's appointment to the Lords in order to take up his government position as hypocritical.Sarwar previously said he would not take a peerage, and both he and Andy Burnham have expressed support for replacing the Lords with a senate of the nations and regions.By appointing another life peer within his first few days in office, is the new PM not sending out a completely contradictory signal?I suppose he could argue that Sarwar is an ally on his agenda to rewire how British politics works and can now agitate for change from within.As an experienced Labour leader in a devolved parliament with previous service as a Westminster MP, Sarwar might be particularly well placed to back up Burnham's case for devolving power from parliaments into cities and communities.Their argument is that this could be a potential driver of economic growth.Who could replace Sarwar?Where does all this leave Scottish Labour? At yet another crossroads.Sarwar was the party's tenth leader since the creation of the Scottish Parliament. They are now looking for number eleven.The current deputy leader Jackie Baillie is in acting charge as she has been on a number of occasions in the past. She has always resisted running for the top job and will almost certainly do so again.More likely candidates include MSP Michael Marra and Sarwar's probable replacement at Holyrood Monica Lennon - if the rules of the contest allow her to run.The snag is that you have to be an MSP to stand for the leadership and she will not be able to take her seat until the Scottish Parliament returns in September.My gut feeling - and it is only that - is that the party's ruling body, the Scottish Executive Committee, would want to accommodate her candidacy if she would like to run.Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, The Scottish Labour party currently has 17 MSPsOther names that have been mentioned include MSPs Daniel Johnson and Paul Sweeney.As I mentioned, there is already an internal debate about what went wrong in the 2026 Holyrood campaign and what the party needs to change. These conversations would fold in to any leadership contest.The choice of personality is obviously important as is the party's future policy offer.There's also likely to be discussion about whether or not Scottish Labour should split from the UK party. Others might want to revisit their opposition to another independence referendum.Huge questions for a party that used to dominate Scottish politics and hungers for a way back to power at Holyrood.

schema.headline: Scottish Labour at a crossroads again as Sarwar jumps ship

title: Most bus fares in England to be capped at £2 from January
description: The bus fare cap in England will revert to £2 from January, new prime minister Andy Burnham has announced.The government says the policy, which is exp
body: The bus fare cap in England will revert to £2 from January, new prime minister Andy Burnham has announced.The government says the policy, which is expected to cost more than £500m, will "help with the cost of living and give people the breathing space they need".At the start of last year, the England-wide bus fare cap rose to £3 under Sir Keir Starmer's Labour government, although in Liverpool and Manchester fares were held at £2.Burnham said affordable transport links were an essential, adding: "Lower fares will help people get to where they need to."The measure gives people "a little bit of hope" in a time when many are grappling with higher living costs - especially after fuel costs spiked as a result of the US-Iran war, transport secretary Heidi Alexander told the BBC's Today programme.However, she admitted the measure was  "not the whole solution".The new cap will take effect from January next year for fares on participating buses outside of London. The cost of a non-discounted single bus journey in London is £1.75.The Burnham government said going back to a £2 cap should particularly help passengers in rural and coastal areas, where single fares can be higher.The Campaign for Better Transport said rural areas were still suffering from fewer bus routes and services, adding that such areas had been more affected by council cut backs in the last decade than urban zones.Services in those areas - as measured by vehicle miles - decreased by 18% between 2019 and 2024, according to the County Councils Network, which represents 39 unitary and county councils across England."What we need to see is the replacing of those lost services which have particularly affected those in rural areas," Campaign for Better Transport's Michael Solomon Williams said.Shadow Chancellor Mel Stride said the government has not actually specified where the money will come from to fund the bus fare cap extension."That is a worrying sign, we need to have a government which is on top of the public finances, not one that is making lots of spending commitments without explaining where the money is going to come from," Stride told BBC Breakfast.In its announcement, the government said £454m will be reallocated from the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESN). DESN says this will be used in addition to funds already earmarked by the Department for Transport for buses.Alexander told the BBC it was money that had been allocated in DESN's budget to spend on international climate projects."And so instead of putting that money out as grants we are going to be issuing loans and so that money comes back in," she said.It is the second day in a row that the new prime minister has announced cost saving measures, following Tuesday's VAT cut on household electricity bills later this year.In his first cabinet meeting on Tuesday, Burnham promised to lead a "cost of living government", telling ministers that he wanted to give people a "sense that help is coming" on costs.

schema.headline: Most bus fares in England to be capped at £2 from January

title: Bangladesh: A year after deadly jet crash, families demand answers
description: It was a normal day for sixth-grade student Nure Jannat Yusha when suddenly, a Bangladeshi air force jet crashed into her school in the capital, Dhaka
body: It was a normal day for sixth-grade student Nure Jannat Yusha when suddenly, a Bangladeshi air force jet crashed into her school in the capital, Dhaka, killing 36 people - most of them children.Within minutes, Milestone School and College, a private institution buzzing with student activity, was transformed into a scene of chaos and raging flames.It was the deadliest aviation disaster the country had seen in decades. Earlier this week, families and survivors gathered to mark one year since the incident - many of them students who say they are struggling to return to normal life, Yusha among them. "Even now, if I go to school and see a plane, I feel afraid. I can't sleep at night. In my dreams, someone is throwing me into the fire," she told BBC Bengali. She was one of more than 160 people who were injured in the crash at Milestone School - and among those at the memorial held at a hospital in Dhaka on Tuesday, where the families of some victims say they are still waiting for answers about the deadly crash. Yusha's mother Yasin Akter says her child had once dreamt of becoming a dancer - an aspiration that has since faded. "My daughter screams all day and is afraid," she says, adding that Yusha has become paranoid about planes falling from the sky."Earlier she was interested in studies. Now she does not want to study any more."On 21 July 2025, a training aircraft took off from a Bangladesh Air Force Base in Dhaka at around 13:00 local time (07:00 GMT) and crashed soon after in the Uttara neighbourhood.Images from the scene hours after the crash showed scores of emergency service workers sorting through charred rubble in an attempt to find survivors.Authorities announced that an investigation commission had been formed to investigate the crash, but in the days that followed, hundreds of student protesters took to the streets, clashing with police as they demanded accountability over the disaster and changes to training flights regulations.The committee's findings, which it reportedly submitted to the government last November, have not yet been made public.According to a copy of the full report obtained by BBC Bengali, it highlighted issues like Milestone School's lack of compliance with building codes. It also found more than 600 buildings - including schools and hospitals - located near Dhaka's international airport that had violated height restrictions.The report also found that the pilot lacked sufficient flight training and proper ground supervision. And there was no technical fault with the aircraft, the report said - contradicting an early statement by the Bangladeshi Armed Forces that the jet had experienced a mechanical fault after taking off.The Bangladesh Armed Forces did not respond to a request for comment."No one can give a guarantee that another plane will not fall onto this school in the future," Mansur Helal, the husband of one victim, told the crowd during the memorial, referencing a recent BBC documentary on the committee report."This is truly a horrific situation. Every day around 2,500-3,000 students go to that school."Helal's wife, schoolteacher Mahreen Chowdhury, had died in hospital from extensive burns she had suffered while rescuing students."The building in which this accident happened had only one door. How can there be only one door for 800-900 students?" Helal added.Helal and others are calling for the government to release the full report. They are also urging better government support for survivors undergoing treatment, as well as compensation for them and victims' families - which was recommended in the investigative committee report but yet to be implemented.Health Minister Sardar Sakhawat Hossain, who was at the event on Tuesday, said the government would support survivors who require further medical treatment. And Zubaida Rahman, the wife of Prime Minister Tarique Rahman, hailed the courage shown by survivors as "a source of inspiration for the nation".But many of the students who attended the memorial this week, sporting burn scars, are still grappling with their new reality.Mehreen, who's in the fifth grade, says she is no longer able to go to school because of her injuries."If I get a little sunlight, my whole body burns. For that reason, I do not go to school any more."It has also affected her sleep and learning ability: "Earlier, if I read something, I would memorise it in two minutes. Now I can't memorise a line in two hours."I can't sleep at night. When I do sleep, I often have strange dreams. I still can't forget that incident."

schema.headline: Bangladesh: A year after deadly jet crash, families demand answers

title: Wildberries: Russia's businesses under strain from Ukraine's attacks on retail warehouses
description: Ukrainian drones have hit several retail warehouses belonging to Russia's biggest online retailer, Wildberries, in a matter of days, dealing a blow to
body: Ukrainian drones have hit several retail warehouses belonging to Russia's biggest online retailer, Wildberries, in a matter of days, dealing a blow to a large number of small and medium-sized businesses.After months of drone attacks on oil depots and refineries, Kyiv says it is now targeting logistics hubs used by Russians to buy military equipment, and disrupting supplies to the Russian army.But it has also frustrated hundreds of Russian sellers who depend on online marketplaces for their livelihoods, making the war feel closer to home after almost four and a half years."I am a mother of a disabled child, I've built the business myself," one woman complained on an expletive-laden social media post that went viral."I've lost 1m roubles (£9,500; $12,700)... We're already getting screwed over from every direction as it is."In Ukraine's first attack on Wildberries at the weekend, two warehouses were hit, at Elektrostal east of Moscow and in Kotovsk in the Tambov region, some 400km (250 miles) from the Ukrainian border.Seven workers on a nightshift were killed in Kotovsk and one was fatally wounded at Elektrostal. Dozens more were injured and goods worth an estimated at £750m ($1bn) were destroyed.Kyiv said it had struck "major logistics facilities" used to "supply sanctioned components for drone production and navigation equipment".Unlike Western retail giant Amazon, which sells goods directly alongside its marketplace business, Wildberries and Ozon act almost entirely as marketplaces, connecting independent sellers with buyers across Russia, storing and delivering their goods and handling payments.This means many of the losses incurred in the attacks have been borne by businesses.For one woman, who sells stationery items, Wildberries is the only distribution channel. The day after she delivered her goods to the Elektrostal warehouse near Moscow last Friday, she learned that it had all gone up in flames.She still has other inventory left, but this time of the year is important for her business, as parents and students stock up for the new autumn term.Another Wildberries trader who sells teenage clothing, told the BBC she had lost about 1.4m roubles (£13,300) worth of stock. She is halting new supplies for a few weeks."The outlook for working with Wildberries is very murky," she said. "Right now I have enough of a safety cushion to survive the current losses, but if there are more strikes, I probably won't manage."Neither woman expects compensation. Earlier this month, less than two weeks before the attacks, a clause was added to their contracts, including drone attacks in the list of "force majeure events" for which Wildberries is not liable.Company owner Tatiana Kim has promised "not to abandon" the sellers, and she has offered temporary discounts and waived shipment fees, but this has done little to calm fears of further attacks.Supporting small and medium-sized businesses has been among Vladimir Putin's declared priorities even in wartime, and framed as a backbone of resilience in the face of Western sanctions.But the Russian state has been unable to shield the sector from the war's impact, and there have been several shocks to businesses since the start of the year.VAT went up from 20% to 22% in January, with extra funding earmarked for defence, and tax breaks for some businesses were scrapped.Some 209,000 small and medium-sized companies closed in the first quarter of 2026, according to business intelligence platform Kontur.Fokus. That is 9% more than in the first three months of 2025.Widespread internet shutdowns and a crackdown on popular messaging apps led to further troubles, and Moscow businesses lost tens of millions of dollars in one week in March, according to some estimates.And then came the fuel crisis, triggered by Ukrainian strikes on oil depots, refineries and supply routes. "There are already a lot of nails, and they keep hammering them in. Is [the attack on Wildberries warehouses] another nail in the coffin? Well, yes, of course," Prof Ruben Eniklopov of Pompeu Fabra University in Barcelona told the BBC.Small businesses in Russia have proved resilient in the past, but inflation is high, the budget deficit is widening, and oil and gas revenues are 23% lower than in the first six months of last year.Russia has vast financial reserves, accumulated in peacetime, but its war in Ukraine is increasingly swallowing resources, starving the civilian economy of growth.Additional reporting by Olga Shamina.

schema.headline: Wildberries: Russia's businesses under strain from Ukraine's attacks on retail warehouses

title: Strait of Hormuz: Trump threatens to target Iran's bridges and power plants if attacks on ship persist
description: President Donald Trump has threatened that the US would destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant any time its forces attack a ship in the Strait of Ho
body: President Donald Trump has threatened that the US would destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant any time its forces attack a ship in the Strait of Hormuz.Potential targets would include ones "located next to, or in," the capital Tehran, he said in a post on Truth Social.The military response would follow any form of attack on shipping in the vital waterway, "whether it be by missile, rocket, drone, or any other device or weapon".His threat follows the US launching strikes on Iran for the 11th consecutive night, with Tehran warning the US against targeting its nuclear facilities.Iranian state media reported that air defences were activated in the capital Tehran. Explosions were also reported in the north-western city of Tabriz, the south-eastern ports of Chabahar and Konarak, and in the southern city of Bushehr, home to Iran's only nuclear power plant.Iran, which has retaliated against the US and its Middle East allies, warned of reprisals after Trump said the US would soon hit an underground nuclear site near Natanz.Kuwait's army said on Wednesday that its air defences had engaged with drone attacks from Iran, while Bahrain and Jordan said they had also intercepted drone and missile strikes.Secretary of State Marco Rubio said on Wednesday that the US would "defend" ships going through the straits getting "blown up". He added that US officials were "open to diplomacy" but Iran did not seem serious about that. The price of oil jumped in response to the latest escalation, reaching $94 on Wednesday. Iran's top negotiator, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, warned the US on Wednesday evening that "the equation of this war is clear: either all or none!"In a region where we do not sell oil, no-one will sell oil. If our security is not ensured, no infrastructure will be safe, and the security of the strait is in the absence of American forces. We have repeatedly said that the situation of the strait will not return to pre-war conditions," Ghalibaf wrote in a post on X.Rubio earlier said Washington was still willing to engage in diplomacy with Tehran, but added that he did not believe Iran was serious about talks. Prior to that, the US president said the next military target could be a complex known as Pickaxe Mountain, an underground nuclear site where Iran is suspected of building an undeclared enrichment facility.Trump also threatened to "take care of" the Houthi rebels if the Iran-backed militant group in Yemen followed through on its threatened blockade of Saudi ports.A Houthi media official had earlier said they were closing the Bab al-Mandab Strait - the Red Sea's southern gateway - to Saudi vessels.The Red Sea has become crucial for oil shipments from Saudi Arabia during the US-Israeli war with Iran, which has led to the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz.Following the Houthi announcement, two tankers which had just loaded Saudi crude bound for China and India at Saudi Arabia's Red Sea port of Yanbu made U-turns on Tuesday, heading instead towards the Suez Canal, British maritime risk management group Vanguard said. The threat from the Houthis risks further disruption to international shipping, with hostilities in the region having already led to global fuel prices fluctuating significantly.Iran and the US signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) in June to halt military operations and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, as well as to reach an agreement to end the war over the following 60 days. But three weeks after it was signed, Trump declared the ceasefire "over" after Iranian attacks on ships in Hormuz, and US strikes in response.The war with Iran has cost the US some $37.5bn (£28bn) so far - a rise of nearly $8bn since the last estimate in May, US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth said on Tuesday.Appearing before the Senate Appropriations Committee, Hegseth said military training would have to be curtailed unless lawmakers approved an urgent funding boost.House Republicans on Wednesday approved a $95bn budget package that mostly would go to funding the Iran war but also includes a Trump-backed bill changing voting regulations in the US. The measure was passed with only Republican support and faces an uphill battle in the narrowly divided Senate. Republican Senator John Thune on Tuesday defended the financial needs by the Trump administration, arguing Iran was "violating international law by blocking the shipping lanes and the Strait of Hormuz" and that Trump was "within his authority"."In the meantime, I think most of us, at least, believe that we ought to support our men and women in uniform and hope and pray that they're successful," he said of the funding request.Trump attended a dignified transfer ceremony on Wednesday in the city of Dover, Delaware, for the bodies of US servicepeople recently killed in the Middle East. He earlier said Iran would pay for American fatalities "many times over".On Tuesday, the Pentagon gave the name of a fourth soldier believed to have been killed in recent days. It said Sgt Angel S Rampersad, 28, who previously was declared missing after an Iranian strike in Jordan, was thought to have died alongside two others in the same strike.

schema.headline: Strait of Hormuz: Trump threatens to target Iran's bridges and power plants if attacks on ship persist

title: CJP protest at Jantar Mantar in Delhi: The meme language of India's Gen Z 'cockroach' protest
description: Indian police cracked down on 'cockroach' protesters. They went home and made memes about itImage source, ReutersImage caption, CJP protesters are res
body: Indian police cracked down on 'cockroach' protesters. They went home and made memes about itImage source, ReutersImage caption, CJP protesters are responding to Monday's police violence with memes onlineByZoya MateenCorrespondentReporting fromDelhiOn Monday, 20-year-old Chhavi (name changed) paused outside a Delhi metro station on her way to what would become one of the largest anti-government demonstrations against Prime Minister Narendra Modi's government in the Indian capital in recent years.She balanced her phone against a railing, stepped back and filmed a quick "fit check" - a video showing off her outfit before the protest."Going to get beaten by cops, guys," she smiled into the camera.Hours later, police fired tear gas and charged demonstrators with batons as tens of thousands of supporters of the Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) – a youth-led movement that grew from protests over exam irregularities into a broader campaign for government accountability – marched towards parliament. They demanded the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan over exam paper leaks and recruitment scandals. Pradhan has refused to resign, accusing the opposition of exploiting students for political gain. Authorities suspended mobile internet around the protest site. By evening, protesters said scores had been beaten by police, while police said several officers had also been injured.When Chhavi finally uploaded the videos that evening, one thing stood out. Before the march, she joked about being beaten by police. Hours later, she was still smiling - posing beside riot officers, flashing victory signs, filming through crowds and even interviewing an injured protester. The clips were among thousands of reels, memes and first-person dispatches that flooded Instagram once protesters regained internet access. Some were raw with anger. Others were breathless, frightened or quietly defiant. Yet it was often the sardonic humour that drew the eye.Image source, Deeva AroraImage caption, A lot of protesters posted "fit checks" - a viral Instagram trend - ahead of the marchOne young woman calmly reapplied her lipstick in front of a line of riot police. Another joked she was "serving face at the protest because the government couldn't serve justice". One reel began as a routine outfit reveal before abruptly cutting to protesters fleeing tear gas. Another gave a deadpan "review" of tear gas, rating its smell and sting like a new restaurant.To many watching online, the humour seemed strangely at odds with the violence. For the young people making the videos, there was no contradiction.None of the protesters interviewed for this story spoke about algorithms, engagement or going viral. They described filming as almost inseparable from protesting itself.And when they turned to humour, it was almost always self-directed: joking about the kilometres logged fleeing baton charges or the absurdity of filming an outfit reveal moments before running into tear gas. The jokes did not erase the fear. They coexisted with it.Experts say the trend hints at a broader shift in how a generation raised online experiences protest."Older generations separated political life from everyday life," said Joyojeet Pal, a professor at the University of Michigan who studies technology and society."Younger generations grow up with politics alongside their memes, their family pictures and their daily commutes."For Chhavi, documenting the protest continued once the crackdown began. Some commenters later accused her of treating the violence as content.Image source, ReutersImage caption, From one-liners to satirical digs, the placards have become one of the defining features of the protests"But I was adrenaline-soaked, scared, petrified," she said. "It was either laugh in the face of fear or cry."That instinct to keep filming, even as the situation turned violent, is one that Payal Arora, a digital anthropologist at Utrecht University, says has become increasingly common around the world. Smartphones have turned ordinary people into chroniclers of political upheaval, recording moments that might otherwise disappear."The act of capturing a protest is often an end in itself," she said. "Their message is more like: 'This happened. We were there. We will remember.'"The humour, too, followed a pattern. No young protester described the day as funny. They spoke instead of tear gas, baton charges and the confusion of watching a peaceful march descend into chaos. People often mistake that humour for detachment, says Nishant Shah, director of Digital Narratives Center at  School of Journalism & Communication at the Chinese University of Hong Kong. In reality, he argues, it serves almost the opposite purpose."Politics is everyday, emotional and experiential," he said.Humour has long been part of protest movements. It can create solidarity, ease tension and help people endure moments of fear."Even when the cause is brutal and the fight fierce," Shah said, "there is joy, laughter, songs and hope. Without those things, protests don't sustain themselves."Ishaan (name changed), 21, came to Delhi for his first protest. When tear gas landed nearby, he reached for his phone instead of leaving.Image source, EPA/ShutterstockImage caption, Police fired tear gas to disperse protesters as they tried to reach parliamentThe reel he posted later showed white smoke drifting across the street beneath a simple caption: "Delhi Police doesn't realise I'm from Jamuna Paar."For most Delhiites, the joke needed little explanation. Jamuna Paar - literally "across the Yamuna" - is shorthand for the city's eastern neighbourhoods and the subject of endless teasing over their neglected civic infrastructure."The smell there is worse than the tear gas," Ishan said, laughing.He told the BBC the joke was never about pretending nothing had happened."It was about refusing to let fear dictate the memory of the day," he said.Protest movements have always developed their own emotional vocabulary to help people endure moments of fear together.India's freedom movement had songs and speeches. Trade unions marched behind banners. Student movements embraced posters and street theatre. More recently, the anti-citizenship protests were marked by murals and public readings of the Constitution, while the farmers' movement became synonymous with tractor rallies and livestreams from Delhi's borders.The CJP protests spoke in the idiom of a generation raised online. To dismiss that language as frivolous, Shah argues, is to misunderstand what it is doing."We often make the mistake of trying to understand new technologies through older ways of thinking," Shah said.Image source, ManitImage caption, Manit even logged a running activity while evading a police baton chargeBut a meme is not simply today's version of a political poster, he said. It gathers meaning as people remake it, parody it and pass it on. Sharing it becomes another way of participating in the protest.That was true for Manit, an 18-year-old engineering student. It was his first protest, and he had almost decided not to film."I was scared," he said. Then he looked around. "Everyone was recording, talking to the camera, just being themselves. So I asked my friend to record me."At first glance, the reel resembled the workout videos he usually posted. Only the final frame revealed the distance on his fitness app had been logged while fleeing police baton charges."I escaped the lathi charge," he laughed. "I ran and ran and ended up completing my workout too."For Manit, that was precisely the point."Not all young people read the news," he said. "If humour can make important conversations accessible, why wouldn't we use it?"*Some names have been changed to protect the identity of the personFollow BBC News India on Instagram, external, YouTube,, external X, external and Facebook, external.

schema.headline: CJP protest at Jantar Mantar in Delhi: The meme language of India's Gen Z 'cockroach' protest

title: Africa's camels are dying from extreme heat as temperatures soar
description: Even camels can't cope: Africa's ships of the desert hit by rising temperaturesImage source, ReutersImage caption, Livestock herders in the Horn of Af
body: Even camels can't cope: Africa's ships of the desert hit by rising temperaturesImage source, ReutersImage caption, Livestock herders in the Horn of Africa and North Africa have switched to camels over the years because of their famed resilienceByNavin Singh KhadkaEnvironment correspondentUpdated 2 hours agoCamels are famously known as the ships of the desert, but even these hardy animals are not being spared from the extremes of rising temperatures in Africa.The Afar region of north-eastern Ethiopia is one of the hottest and driest places on Earth, where nomads often lead camel caravans carrying salt through the desert."I can see my camels suffering from extreme heat," Ali Umer, a camel herder from Semera in Afar, told the BBC."They get blisters on their feet, their eyes become watery and the hot sand burns their skin and eats up their hair when they are sitting," said the 40-year-old who owns around 500 camels."Within the past one month I have lost eight camel calves because of the extreme heat, it has become much more intense and difficult."Even the wind, which used to bring cool air, now carries heat," he said, adding he had noticed these drastic changes in the temperature in recent years.He is not alone in his observations: scientists, animal welfare workers and various studies all mention stress, fatigue, dehydration, disease prevalence as direct impacts of extreme heat.The higher temperatures also indirectly make camels suffer by causing severe loss of water, vegetation, and reduced shaded areas.Image source, Corbis via Getty ImagesImage caption, Camels carry bricks of salt cut from the mineral-laden lakes in the Danakil Depression, where temperatures can top 50CNorth Africa and the Horn of Africa host around 80% of the world's largest population of dromedary camels - those with one hump.Over the years, farmers and cattle herders in these regions have switched to these animals because of their resilience to harsh heat and drought conditions.But a study based on the perception of camel herders in southern Ethiopia, published in 2025, , externalshowed that high temperatures and climate variability had had a detrimental impact on camel herd health."Respondents reported that climate change and variability have affected camels' health, productivity, and reproductive performance," it said.In neighbouring Somalia, the situation appears to be more serious. Research published in June, external has shown that drought-related camel mortality is a widespread crisis, affecting nearly 46% of camel-owning households.The study included nearly 6,000 pastoral households and showed that the mortality was concentrated in northern regions, with Sool reporting a "staggering mortality rate of nearly 73%".Figure caption, Two of Ali Umer's shepherds in Ethiopia's Afar region bathe a one-month-old camel to cool it downExtreme heat is the main cause behind the drought in the region although other factors like inadequate veterinary services and animal management make things worse, experts say.An analysis by atmospheric scientists, external found that high temperatures in the region were behind the 2021-22 drought, as extreme heat makes water from soil and water bodies turn into vapour and plants release water in them into air as vapour.Veterinarians say they are treating more sick camels and are seeing more deaths due to extreme heat and drought."We are seeing significant increase in cases of hyperthermia in camels as a result of which they get multiple infections," Hassan Nuya Guyo, a veterinary officer in Marsabit county in north-eastern Kenya bordering Ethiopia, told the BBC."In the last two years, we have seen a more than 15% increase in camel deaths in our county due to extreme heat."Disease prevalence among camels and reduced milk yield from them are as a result of climate change impacts including heat stress, according to another study conducted in the Horn of Africa, external.Image source, Hassan Nuya GuyoImage caption, Hassan Nuya Guyo, a vet in north-eastern Kenya, says in the last two years there has been a 15% increase in camel deaths in the area he overseesA similar story is playing out in North Africa. A study in Algeria, external, published this year, has shown a significant increase in camel deaths during summer heatwaves. "Time series analysis highlights significant seasonal mortality patterns, underscoring the need for targeted interventions during high-risk periods like peak summer months; such as strategic water provisioning, supplemental feeding, and enhanced disease monitoring," it said.The World Meteorological Organization (WMO) says extreme heat events have become a common phenomenon in several North African countries with heatwaves having increased with a clear upward trend since 1981.It said a number of countries in the region and the Middle East reported temperatures above 50C in 2024.WMO's State of the Climate in Africa 2025 report, external said North Africa recorded the most rapid warming of the six subregions - 0.43C per decade - between 1991 and 2025. Experts say camels comfortably beat the heat up to around 40C by mainly allowing their body temperature to fluctuate by several degrees throughout the day to deal with outside temperature.By doing that they avoid sweating and, combined with concentrated urine and dry faeces, it helps them minimise water loss, while their thick skin coat helps them ward off solar radiation.Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Experts say camels comfortably beat the heat up to around 40C by mainly allowing their body temperature to fluctuate by several degrees throughout the day"But camels can also experience heat stress when temperatures rise beyond their comfort range," said Dr Mohammed Bengoumi, a scientist with the UN's Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), who specialises in camel health and works in North Africa."In many parts of the Sahara, summer temperatures now frequently exceed 50C, increasing the risk of severe heat stress, particularly when accompanied by reduced vegetation cover, water scarcity, and limited access to shade," he told the BBC.Professor Abdelmadjid Chehma at the University of Ghardaia in Algeria, who has conducted experimental works on camels in the Sahara Desert, has a similar conclusion."Our works show that prolonged day-and-night heat above 41 to 45C directly attack their cells,"  he told the BBC."Their immune system suffers because such heat levels damage their white blood cells and other cells because of stress, they [the cells] struggle to repair themselves and end up destroying themselves."For camels this means when heat stress sets in, their metabolism slows down to limit internal heat production leading to sharp reduction in food intake resulting in lethargy and generalised fatigue.That leads to drop in milk production and also causes reproductive disorders.Image source, AFP via Getty ImagesImage caption, Pastoralists who rely on camels for milk are suffering as studies show that heat stress has reduced yieldsProfessor Mohammed el Khasmi, animal hydrobiology expert at the University of Casablanca in Morocco, has a long list of effects of heat stress he and his team have observed on camels.Weight loss, inflammation, muscle and bone related illnesses, chemical imbalance due to kidney malfunction, infectious diseases caused by parasites were a few among them, he said.While extreme heat is the primary culprit, its impacts become severe when other factors like loss of vegetation and water, lack of shaded areas and inadequate veterinary services add up, experts say.This is why many herders in Ethiopia have moved their camels from the northern region, where the animals were found historically, to the south where water and vegetation are relatively available, experts there say."And this is not the seasonal movement of the herders," said Daniel Gelan, professor and pastoralist development researcher with Vision University in Ethiopia.Image source, Ali UmerImage caption, Ali Umer says that within the last month he has lost eight camel calves because of the extreme heat"They are leaving the northern region for good because they can no longer sustain camel herding there because of extreme heat and drought," he told the BBC.The Horn of Africa has been recovering from the longest and most severe recorded drought spanning from 2020 to 2023, but now it is seeing another round of the harsh condition, according to charity organisation Oxfam.It said water prices in some parts of Somalia had risen by more than 2,000% because of the drought.Umer, the cattle herder in Afar, said he was worried that an outbreak of disease could start killing his adult camels anytime now.His concern is valid, according to Prof Chehma."While camels remain the livestock herders' last line of defence against the desert, the unprecedented intensity of current heatwaves directly threatens their vital functions, turning this historically resilient animal into a climate victim," he said.Additional reporting by BBC Amharic's Firehiwot KassaYou may also be interested in:

schema.headline: Africa's camels are dying from extreme heat as temperatures soar

title: The BBC News app keeps you informed with live and breaking news you can trust
description: Depending on the contract you have, data charges may apply for accessing the internet on your mobile device.If you are not sure about the potential ch
body: Depending on the contract you have, data charges may apply for accessing the internet on your mobile device.If you are not sure about the potential charges, please ask your mobile network provider. You may find some costs are included in your existing price plan or that you can opt for a data package that gives reduced charges for accessing the internet.The BBC does not charge you to access mobile content.

schema.headline: The BBC News app keeps you informed with live and breaking news you can trust

title: Mamdani backs off  pledge to arrest Netanyahu citing lack of authority
description: New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani, who previously pledged to arrest Benjamin Netanyahu if he visited the city, now says he does not have the "authori
body: New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani, who previously pledged to arrest Benjamin Netanyahu if he visited the city, now says he does not have the "authority" to do so.In a video posted on social media, Mamdani instead called on US officials to act on the International Criminal Court's arrest warrant for the Israeli Prime Minister over alleged war crimes. Technically, if Netanyahu set foot in an ICC member state, he could be arrested and handed over to the court - but the US is not a member of the ICC. President Donald Trump has said Netanyahu would not be "arrested, in any way, shape or form" while in the US."He is fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran, which recently killed 52,000 innocent protestors [sic], and has spent the last 47 years killing American Soldiers, and others," Trump said in a Truth Social post on Monday.Trump was responding to comments Mamdani made in an interview with the New York Times on Saturday, in which the mayor said he was having "an active conversation" with law enforcement about the possibility of enforcing the ICC arrest warrant.But on Tuesday, Mamdani said his team had reviewed "every avenue available" under the law, and concluded that "we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this"."The federal government, however, does," Mamdani stated. "And I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant."Mamdani said in the social media video that Netanyahu was "not welcome" in New  York City, where he is expected to travel in September for the UN General Assembly.The ICC issued an arrest warrant for Netanyahu in 2024 over alleged war crimes in Gaza, while also issuing an arrest warrant for a Hamas commander.The ICC has the authority to prosecute those accused of genocide, crimes against humanity and war crimes on the territory of states party to the Rome Statute, its founding treaty. During his campaign for New York City mayor, Mamdani repeatedly called Netanyahu a war criminal, and the war in Gaza a genocide. He also said he would order police to arrest Netanyahu if he arrived in the city.Speaking then to the New York Times as a candidate, he said: "This is something that I intend to fulfill." He said that such an arrest would be honouring a warrant issued by the ICC.His remarks come as Democratic voters' support for Israel appears to be shifting, with a recent poll , externalsuggesting 58% of Democrats believe the US is too supportive of Israel, up from 45% in 2024. In response to Mamdani's interview, Danny Danon, the Israeli ambassador to the UN said to the mayor: "You were elected to serve New Yorkers, not Hamas' propaganda."

schema.headline: Mamdani backs off  pledge to arrest Netanyahu citing lack of authority
